ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a fried tofu food product class; the peanut sauce component is not separately encoded. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers fried tofu; peanut sauce accompaniment not separately represented. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for tahu goreng with peanut sauce. No code assigned.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of tahu goreng with peanut sauce is approximately 200 g, comprising several pieces of deep-fried tofu topped with a generous portion of peanut sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Tahu goreng (fried tofu with peanut sauce) — composite estimate derived from HPB Singapore Foodo fried tofu entry and USDA FoodData Central peanut sauce / satay sauce reference values
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) Foodo; USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo provides a fried tofu (tahu goreng) entry used as the base. Peanut sauce macros were supplemented using USDA FoodData Central reference for peanut-based sauce (satay sauce). A 70:30 tofu-to-sauce ratio by weight was applied to derive composite per-100g values, consistent with typical Singaporean tahu goreng preparation. Values are estimates and should be verified against a laboratory analysis of the specific restaurant preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of soybean-based foods and tofu products — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for tofu and soy-based foods; peanut sauce contribution modelled from published GI of peanuts (GI ~14) and mixed-dish blending methodology
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for tahu goreng with peanut sauce was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 40 using a weighted blend of: (1) fried tofu GI ~35 (University of Sydney GI Database, soy-based food entries), and (2) peanut sauce GI ~14 (peanuts, University of Sydney GI Database). A 70:30 tofu-to-sauce ratio was applied. The resulting blended GI of approximately 40 is classified as low (≤55). Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (40 × 9.8 × 2.0) / 100 = 7.84, rounded to 7.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~620 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful that a standard 200 g serving of tahu goreng with peanut sauce contributes approximately 620 mg sodium, representing about 27% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association; portion control and requesting reduced-sauce options are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40) and moderate carbohydrate content (~9.8 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tahu goreng with peanut sauce has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 40 and a glycemic load of approximately 7.8 per standard serving, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions; however, the peanut sauce may add sugar and should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat (~13.5 g per 100 g) with moderate saturated fat (~2.8 g per 100 g); beneficial soy isoflavones from tofu
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While tahu goreng provides heart-beneficial soy protein and isoflavones from tofu, the deep-frying process and peanut sauce contribute moderate total fat and sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and prefer preparations with less oil and reduced-sodium sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tahu goreng with peanut sauce is a popular Southeast Asian dish, particularly common in Indonesian and Singaporean cuisine, consisting of firm tofu that has been deep-fried until golden and crispy on the outside while remaining soft and silky on the inside, served topped with a rich, savoury-sweet peanut sauce. The peanut sauce is typically made from ground roasted peanuts blended with garlic, chilli, soy sauce, sugar, and sometimes tamarind or lime juice, giving it a complex flavour profile that balances nuttiness, sweetness, saltiness, and mild heat. The dish is commonly served as a starter, side dish, or light main course in hawker centres, food courts, and casual restaurants across Singapore and Indonesia. Nutritionally, tahu goreng with peanut sauce is a moderate-calorie dish (approximately 195 kcal per 100 g) that provides a meaningful source of plant-based protein (~9.5 g per 100 g) from tofu, along with healthy fats from peanuts. The dish has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 40, making it relatively suitable for blood glucose management. Sodium content is moderate to high (~310 mg per 100 g), which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension. The tofu component contributes calcium, iron, and soy isoflavones, which may have cardiovascular benefits. This dish is suitable for vegetarians and vegans and is generally halal. It is a good example of a soy-based protein dish that balances flavour and nutrition in the context of Southeast Asian dietary patterns.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tahu goreng with peanut sauce?
Is tahu goreng suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of fried tofu?
What allergens are in tahu goreng?
How much sodium is in tahu goreng?
What is the difference between tahu goreng and tauhu goreng?
Can I eat tahu goreng if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from a composite estimate using HPB Singapore Foodo fried tofu data and USDA FoodData Central peanut sauce reference values with an assumed 70:30 tofu-to-sauce ratio. No direct laboratory analysis of this specific restaurant preparation is available. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for tofu and peanuts. Confidence is medium rather than high because exact match data for this composite dish is not available in a single approved primary source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates based on HPB Foodo fried tofu and USDA FoodData Central peanut sauce data at a 70:30 ratio; laboratory verification is recommended. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this composite dish in the source databases used. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported for tofu-based dishes in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for this preparation. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this composite dish. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish containing no animal products; confirmed zero by nature of ingredients. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as tofu and peanut sauce are plant-derived; naturally zero. 7. GI is an analogous estimate of 40 based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for fried tofu (~35) and peanuts (~14) blended at 70:30 ratio; gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55). 8. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' to 'Meat and alternatives' as tahu goreng is a soy-protein-based dish. 9. The cuisine_tags in the input (Ramen, Pizza, Pasta) appear to be erroneous restaurant-level tags from field survey and have not been carried forward. 10. Ontology: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03302710 is used as closest parent for fried tofu food product; FoodEx2 code A0CKP is used as closest parent for fried tofu. Both are closest_parent matches as no exact composite code exists for tahu goreng with peanut sauce.
